PTSDdiag 0.2.7

Joining diagnoses back to demographics with id_col

Source: vignettes/id_column_workflow.Rmd
id_column_workflow.Rmd

The problem

The PTSDdiag workflow normalises your dataframe to a canonical symptom_1 through symptom_20 form before optimisation. Demographics (patient_id, age, sex, …) are dropped at that step, so the optimised diagnosis cannot be joined back to the original dataframe to answer questions like “do participants meeting the optimised criterion differ in age from those who don’t?”

This vignette shows how to thread a participant identifier through the workflow using the id_col argument so that per-row diagnoses can be merged back to demographics.

Set up a dataframe with demographics 

library(PTSDdiag)
library(dplyr)

set.seed(123)
n <- 200
study_df <- data.frame(
  patient_id = sprintf("P%03d", seq_len(n)),
  age        = sample(20:70, n, replace = TRUE),
  sex        = sample(c("F", "M"), n, replace = TRUE),
  simulated_ptsd[seq_len(n), ],
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
head(study_df[, 1:6])
#>   patient_id age sex S1 S2 S3
#> 1       P001  50   F  2  1  4
#> 2       P002  34   M  3  3  4
#> 3       P003  70   F  0  1  1
#> 4       P004  33   F  3  3  3
#> 5       P005  22   F  2  3  3
#> 6       P006  61   M  1  1  2

Carry the ID through rename_ptsd_columns()

Pass the column name(s) of any participant-level fields you want to preserve. The simplest pattern is to use a single participant identifier and join everything else back at the end:

sym <- rename_ptsd_columns(study_df[, c("patient_id", names(simulated_ptsd))],
                           id_col = "patient_id")
names(sym)[1:5]
#> [1] "patient_id" "symptom_1"  "symptom_2"  "symptom_3"  "symptom_4"

sym now contains patient_id plus symptom_1 through symptom_20. age and sex stay in study_df and will be joined back via patient_id at the end. If you wanted them to ride through the workflow as well, pass them too: id_col = c("patient_id", "age", "sex").

Optimise — the ID rides along 

res <- optimize_combinations(
  sym, n_symptoms = 6, n_required = 4, n_top = 2,
  show_progress = FALSE
)
head(res$diagnosis_comparison)
#>   patient_id PTSD_orig symptom_2_6_7_11_12_15 symptom_2_6_7_11_12_19
#> 1       P001      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 2       P002      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 3       P003      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 4       P004      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 5       P005      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 6       P006      TRUE                  FALSE                   TRUE

res$diagnosis_comparison carries patient_id as its first column. Every diagnostic column (PTSD_orig, the optimised combinations) is aligned to the same patient_id.

Join diagnoses back to demographics 

final <- study_df %>%
  select(patient_id, age, sex) %>%
  left_join(res$diagnosis_comparison, by = "patient_id")
head(final)
#>   patient_id age sex PTSD_orig symptom_2_6_7_11_12_15 symptom_2_6_7_11_12_19
#> 1       P001  50   F      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 2       P002  34   M      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 3       P003  70   F      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 4       P004  33   F      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 5       P005  22   F      TRUE                   TRUE                   TRUE
#> 6       P006  61   M      TRUE                  FALSE                   TRUE

Now you can compare demographics by diagnostic status. The column generated by the best optimised combination is named after its symptom indices (e.g. symptom_3_6_10_15_17_19); pick the first non-PTSD_orig diagnostic column for the demonstration:

combo_col <- setdiff(grep("^symptom_", names(final), value = TRUE),
                     paste0("symptom_", 1:20))[1]

final %>%
  group_by(.data[[combo_col]]) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), mean_age = mean(age), pct_female = mean(sex == "F"))
#> # A tibble: 2 × 4
#>   symptom_2_6_7_11_12_15     n mean_age pct_female
#>   <lgl>                  <int>    <dbl>      <dbl>
#> 1 FALSE                     13     48.4      0.692
#> 2 TRUE                     187     45.8      0.588

The same trick works for validation

The ID rides through holdout_validation() and cross_validation() too — useful because those functions reshuffle rows and positional cbind would silently misalign your data.

ho <- holdout_validation(sym, train_ratio = 0.7, seed = 42,
                         n_symptoms = 6, n_required = 4, n_top = 2)
head(ho$without_clusters$test_results)
#>    patient_id PTSD_orig symptom_1_2_6_9_12_13 symptom_1_2_9_12_13_20
#> 7        P007      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 8        P008      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 10       P010      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 11       P011      TRUE                 FALSE                  FALSE
#> 17       P017      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 19       P019      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE

Each row of test_results carries the patient_id it came from, so a left_join from demographics recovers the test fold exactly.

study_df %>%
  select(patient_id, age, sex) %>%
  inner_join(ho$without_clusters$test_results, by = "patient_id") %>%
  head()
#>   patient_id age sex PTSD_orig symptom_1_2_6_9_12_13 symptom_1_2_9_12_13_20
#> 1       P007  69   M      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 2       P008  62   M      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 3       P010  33   M      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 4       P011  44   M      TRUE                 FALSE                  FALSE
#> 5       P017  47   M      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE
#> 6       P019  48   M      TRUE                  TRUE                   TRUE

Reserved column names

A small set of column names is reserved by the workflow and cannot be used as id_col: symptom_1 through symptom_20, total, PTSD_orig, PTSD_icd11, PTSD_caps5, PTSD_pcl5, .strata, and any name matching the combination-output pattern (e.g. symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19). Rename your column before calling rename_ptsd_columns() if it collides.

On this page
